skill.name: rust-async-patterns
skill.description: Master Rust async programming with Tokio, async traits, error handling, and concurrent patterns. Use when building async Rust applications, implementing concurrent systems, or debugging async code.
skill.summary: Guides Rust developers through practical async patterns using Tokio, channels, streams, traits, tracing, and graceful shutdown.
skill.author: sickn33

security_audit.summary: Static analysis flagged many external command and obfuscation patterns, but review found they are markdown examples and Rust async task APIs, not executable skill behavior. The remaining concern is low-risk documentation of network URLs, HTTP error types, and database access examples. No evidence found of prompt injection, credential exfiltration, malicious command execution, or hidden instructions.

low_findings.title: Documentation Includes Network And Database Examples
low_findings.description: The playbook includes an example API URL, HTTP client error types, and SQL database query examples. These are instructional snippets, not executable skill code, but users should review generated network and database code before use.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited lines clearly contain network-oriented examples and external resource links. The context confirms they are documentation examples, so the security impact is limited.

low_findings.title: False Positive External Command Detection
low_findings.description: The scanner treated Rust async task spawning and markdown formatting as external command execution. The reviewed lines use tokio::spawn, JoinSet::spawn, code fences, inline code formatting, and skill resource references; they do not run operating system commands.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Review of the reported lines shows Rust async APIs and markdown syntax rather than shell execution. No command strings, process APIs, or user-controlled command arguments were found.

low_findings.title: False Positive Obfuscation, Reconnaissance, And Weak Crypto Signals
low_findings.description: The flagged obfuscation, system reconnaissance, and weak cryptographic algorithm signals are caused by markdown headings, YAML description text, SQL examples, and tracing examples. No evidence found of encoded payloads, system probing, or cryptographic implementation.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited content is plain markdown and Rust sample code with no hidden payload or cryptographic routine. The weak crypto signal appears to be a keyword collision in the skill description.

content.value_statement: Rust async code can fail through unclear task ownership, cancellation gaps, and weak error handling. This skill provides practical Tokio patterns for concurrent services, streams, channels, and debugging.
actual_capabilities: Explains Tokio task spawning, JoinSet usage, and bounded concurrency patterns.
Provides examples for mpsc, broadcast, oneshot, and watch channels.
Shows async error handling with anyhow, thiserror, timeouts, and custom errors.
Documents graceful shutdown with CancellationToken, signal handling, and broadcast channels.
Covers async traits, streams, resource pools, tracing, and tokio-console debugging.
limitations: Does not execute, compile, or test Rust code by itself.
Examples use placeholder types such as Response, Entity, Connection, and PgPool.
Network and database snippets need project-specific security review before production use.
Focuses on Tokio-style Rust async patterns, not every Rust runtime.

use_cases.title: Design Concurrent Backend Workflows
use_cases.description: Plan bounded task execution, channel communication, and shutdown behavior for a Rust service.

use_cases.title: Debug Async Runtime Behavior
use_cases.description: Use tracing, spans, and tokio-console guidance to inspect task execution and runtime issues.

use_cases.title: Teach Rust Async Patterns
use_cases.description: Create learning material that explains futures, streams, channels, and async error handling.

prompt_templates.title: Explain A Pattern
prompt_templates.scenario: Beginner learning a Rust async concept.
prompt_templates.prompt: Explain when to use Tokio mpsc, broadcast, oneshot, and watch channels. Include simple examples and tradeoffs.

prompt_templates.title: Review Async Code
prompt_templates.scenario: Developer wants feedback on a Rust async function.
prompt_templates.prompt: Review this Rust async code for task ownership, cancellation, error handling, and blocking calls. Suggest concrete fixes.

prompt_templates.title: Design A Service Flow
prompt_templates.scenario: Team is planning a concurrent Rust backend.
prompt_templates.prompt: Design a Tokio-based service flow with bounded concurrency, graceful shutdown, tracing, and structured error handling.

prompt_templates.title: Diagnose Runtime Issues
prompt_templates.prompt: Analyze these symptoms in a Tokio service and propose a debugging plan using tracing, spans, tokio-console, and timeout boundaries.

output_examples.input: How should I fetch many URLs without overloading the service?
output_examples.output: Use bounded concurrency with a stream buffer. Set a limit, collect successful results, log failures, and make the limit configurable.

output_examples.input: What should a shutdown plan include for a Tokio server?
output: Give tasks a bounded cleanup window.

output_examples.input: How do I choose an async channel type?
output_examples.output: Use mpsc for work queues, broadcast for event fanout, oneshot for single replies, and watch for shared latest-state updates.

best_practices: Use bounded concurrency when spawning tasks for external work.
Propagate cancellation and avoid holding locks across await points.
Instrument async functions with tracing before debugging runtime behavior.
anti_patterns: Spawning unbounded tasks without backpressure or join management.
Using blocking operations inside async functions without isolation.
Ignoring task errors, cancellation paths, or timeout boundaries.

faq.question: Does this skill run commands or modify files?
faq.answer: No. It is documentation that guides responses about Rust async programming.

faq.question: Which Rust runtime does it focus on?
faq.answer: It focuses on Tokio and related crates such as futures, async-trait, tracing, and tokio-console.

faq.question: Can it generate production-ready code?
faq.answer: It can guide production patterns, but generated code still needs project-specific review, compilation, and tests.

faq.question: Does it cover async database access?
faq.answer: Yes. It includes examples using sqlx-style async repository patterns.

faq.question: Can it help with performance debugging?
faq.answer: Yes. It includes guidance for tracing instrumentation and tokio-console runtime inspection.

faq.question: Is it useful for beginners?
faq.answer: Yes. It starts with core async concepts and then moves into practical service patterns.
